claims.claim_text: A field experiment measured a substantial winter-wheat grain-yield penalty for wheat grown after wheat (and in monoculture) compared with wheat grown after a break crop, confirming that cereal-after-cereal rotation reduces yield.
claims.evidence_text: Wheat grain yield varied significantly with W1 (7.1 ± 2.2 Mg dm ha−1) > WM (6.1 ± 1.8 Mg dm ha−1) > W2 (5.4 ± 2.1 Mg dm ha−1), where W1 is the first wheat after oilseed rape, W2 the second wheat, and WM continuous wheat monoculture.

value.raw_value_text: wheat after wheat ~5.4-6.1 vs ~7.1 Mg/ha after a break crop

provenance.source_title: Effect of crop rotational position and nitrogen supply on root development and yield formation of winter wheat

claims.claim_text: A field experiment measured winter-wheat grain yield roughly 1.7 Mg/ha higher after oilseed rape than after wheat, quantifying the break-crop benefit of an oilseed before a cereal.
claims.evidence_text: grain yield across all years was approximately 1.7 Mg ha−1 higher for wheat grown after oilseed rape than for wheat grown after wheat.

value.raw_value_text: ~1.7 Mg/ha higher than wheat after wheat

provenance.source_title: Effect of crop rotational position and nitrogen supply on root development and yield formation of winter wheat
